coredata.dc:title: A label-free electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of cardiac marker using graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
coredata.dc:creator: Tuteja, Satish K.
Chen, Rui
Kukkar, Manil
Song, Chung Kil
Mutreja, Ruchi
Singh, Suman
Paul, Ashok K.
Lee, Haiwon
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Deep, Akash
Suri, C. Raman
coredata.dc:description: A label-free immunosensor based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy has been developed for the sensitive detection of a cardiac biomarker myoglobin (cMyo). Hydrothermally synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used as an immobilized template on screen printed electrodes for the construction of an impedimetric sensor platform. The GQDs-modified electrode was conjugated with highly specific anti-myoglobin antibodies to develop the desired immunosensor. The values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) were monitored as a function of varying antigen concentration. The Rct value of the immunosensor showed a linear increase (from 0.20 to 0.31kΩ) in the range of 0.01–100ng/mL cMyo. The specific detection of cMyo was also made in the presence of other competing proteins. The limit of detection for the proposed immunosensor was estimated as 0.01ng/mL which is comparable to the standard ELISA techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Myoglobin
Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
Label-free immunosensor
Cardiac marker

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modification of screen printed electrodes (SPEs) with GQDs and their subsequent bioconjugation with anti-cMyo antibody
Characteristics of the generated anti-cMyo antibodies
UV–vis, FTIR, HR-TEM, FE-SEM, particle size and AFM characterizations of the synthesized GQDs
FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, XRD analysis and contact angle measurement
Electrochemical characteristics of the of the synthesized GQDs
Characteristics of anti-cMyo antibodies bioconjugated GQDs
Electrochemical behavior of screen printed electrodes during different steps of immunosensor development
EIS detection of cardiac myoglobin (cMyo) with anti-myoglobin conjugated GQD/SPE
Analysis of myoglobin in spiked human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present research work has been funded through CSIR , India grant ( OMEGA/PSC0202/2.2.5 ) from CSIR, India. Satish Kumar Tuteja also thanks the CSIR, India his senior research fellowship. We are grateful to the Director, CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh, India. Online available Supporting Information contains details on materials, equipment, generation/purification/characterization of anti-cMyo antibodies, synthesis of graphene quantum dots, electrochemical measurements, the electrochemical behavior of screen printed electrodes during different steps of immunosensor development, investigation on the specificity of the developed immunosensor, and stability and regeneration of the developed immunosensor; along with Figs. S1-S11 and Tables S1-S3 . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs along with their photoluminescence characteristics at an excitation energy of 340nm and (b) PL spectra of GQDs at different excitation energy.
(a) TEM image, (b) magnified HR-TEM image, (c) Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern, and (d) Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern of GQDs.
(a) AFM imaging and line profile analysis of the GQD sample showing the formation of nanoparticles of less than 10nm height, (b) FTIR spectrum of GQDs, and (c) Raman spectra of graphite and GQDs.
(a) Cyclic Voltamogramme of graphite and GQDs, inset showing linear sweep voltammetry curve for graphite and GQDs and (b) Chronoamperometry curve for the deposition of GQDs on graphite electrode.

(a) Nyquist plots and values of Rct during the different development stages of immunosensor and analysis of target analyte, cMyo at varying concentrations, (b) Linear range of cMyo detection with the variations in the Rct values as a function of analyte concentration, (c) Specificity test with interfering proteins, namely BSA, Troponin I, Avidin, Hemoglobin and CK-MB and (d) EIS response of the sensor during the analysis of serum samples spiked with different concentrations of cMyo.
(a) DPV of Ab/GQD/SPE immunosensor in presence of cMyo (b) DPV as a function of cMyo concentration; Inset-linearity graph.

head.ce:title: A label-free electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of cardiac marker using graphene quantum dots (GQDs)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Scientific Instruments Organisation (CSIR-CSIO), Sector 30-C, Chandigarh, 160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, CSIR-CSIO, Sector 30-C, Chandigarh, 160030, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, CSIR-CSIO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Institute of Microbial Technology (CSIR-IMTECH), Sector 39, Chandigarh, 160038, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, 133-791, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering & Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul, 133-791, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil Engineering & Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Central Scientific Instruments Organisation (CSIR-CSIO), Sector 30-C, Chandigarh 160030, India.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: DAV University, Jalandhar, Punjab, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A label-free immunosensor based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy has been developed for the sensitive detection of a cardiac biomarker myoglobin (cMyo). Hydrothermally synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used as an immobilized template on screen printed electrodes for the construction of an impedimetric sensor platform. The GQDs-modified electrode was conjugated with highly specific anti-myoglobin antibodies to develop the desired immunosensor. The values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) were monitored as a function of varying antigen concentration. The Rct value of the immunosensor showed a linear increase (from 0.20 to 0.31kΩ) in the range of 0.01–100ng/mL cMyo. The specific detection of cMyo was also made in the presence of other competing proteins. The limit of detection for the proposed immunosensor was estimated as 0.01ng/mL which is comparable to the standard ELISA techniques.

ce:section.ce:para: The acute myocardial infarction (AMI), or more commonly known as the heart attack, happens when the arteries leading to the heart are blocked and the blood supply is slowed down or stopped to a portion of the myocardium (Thygesen et al., 2007). Prolonged and irreversible blockage of oxygen and nutrients in the blood supply (ischemia) may ultimately lead to myocardial cell death and necrosis. As per the WHO guidelines, a patient may be suspected of AMI upon the realization of at least two out of the following three symptoms: characteristic chest pain, changes in electrocardiogram (ECG), and elevation of the cardiac biomarkers in blood sample (Yang and Min Zhou, 2006). Commonly tested cardiac biomarkers include myoglobin (cMyo), creatine kinase-MB (CK-MB), cardiac troponin I (cTnI), and cardiac Troponin T (cTnT) (McDonnell et al., 2009).

The sensitive, specific, and early detection of a definitive cardiac biomarker in the blood is essential for the proper treatment of AMI within as early as 6h from the onset of symptoms (Morrow et al., 2007; Wu et al., 1999). Myoglobin is known as one of the most useful cardiac biomarkers for an early detection of AMI. A short half-life of cMyo due to its relatively low molecular weight (17.8kDa), small molecular dimensions (3nm×4nm×5nm) and small molecular volume (1.87×104 cm3 mol−1) adds the specificity for diagnosis over the short period, e.g, within 24h (Chiu et al., 1999). Its normal concentration levels in the blood (30–90ng/mL) increases quickly upon the onset of AMI (within as early as 1h) and reaches to a maximum (∼200ng/mL) in between 6 and 12h before returning to base value within 24h (Azzazy and Christenson, 2002; de Winter et al., 1995; Karras and Kane, 2001; Mishra et al., 2014; Sallach et al., 2004; Woo et al., 1995). Hence, the level of cMyo in the blood stream is considered a highly valuable parameter for an early diagnostic confirmation of AMI. In addition, its detection is also valuable for “ruling out” the usual symptoms as AMI if not accompanied by the elevation of cMyo (Hasić et al., 2006).

ELISA-based standard assays for cardiac markers provide sensitive detections with a common limit of detection (LOD) of ≥10pg/mL. However, these techniques require lab-scale expensive instrumentation, highly skilled technicians, large sample and reagent consumption, multistep processing, and long duration for analysis. These limitations can be one of particular concerns in the case of AMI diagnosis, as it demands quick clinical decisions for timely and effective treatment of the patients (Zhang et al., 2011). Rapid, reliable, and ‘point-of-care (POC)’ diagnostic solutions are very desirable for the management of AMI medical cases (Soper et al., 2006; Wan et al., 2013). Most of such devices exploit electrochemical transduction techniques due to easy upscaling and effective integration on microchips (Chin et al., 2007; Sia and Kricka, 2008; Yager et al., 2008). Impedimetric immunosensing approach is an important class of electrochemical devices that can facilitate the development of simple, fast, inexpensive, sensitive and specific detection without requiring mediators or enzymes as labels (Daniels and Pourmand, 2007; K’Owino and Sadik, 2005; Katz and Willner, 2003; Mantzila et al., 2008). The application of advanced nanostructures has greatly contributed to the realization of highly effective impedimetric immunosensors for various analytes (Bhalla et al., 2012).

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero dimensional nanostructures from the carbon family with many unique properties, viz. large surface area, zero-blinking, non-toxicity, chemical inertness, low synthesis cost, excellent aqueous solubility, and variable bandgap energy. GQDs have attracted immense interests in different research areas including sensors, optoelectronics, photovoltaics, electroluminescence devices, light-emitting diodes, biological imaging, drug delivery, and biosensing (Li et al., 2011; Pan et al., 2010b; Sun et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2011). Several studies have reported the synthesis of GQDs with different techniques including high-resolution electron-beam, chemical, electrochemical, and hydrothermal processing (Dong et al., 2012; Shin et al., 2014). Most of such approaches relied upon the use of graphene oxide as the starting material (Ponomarenko et al., 2008). Similar to graphene, the GQDs provide excellent opportunity to graft them with various functional groups through π-π conjugated network. The co-presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at their edge can manifest excellent water solubility and easy conjugation with biological moieties. As such, many biosensing applications of GQDs already confirmed their usefulness in achieving improved detection sensitivities (Ryu et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2013).

In this research, we aim to devise an immunosensor for the highly sensitive detection of cardiac biomarker myoglobin. To this end, we carried out the synthesis of high purity GQDs from the waste graphite electrode of spent alkaline battery followed by their conjugation with a cMyo specific antibody. The detection of cMyo has been demonstrated in both the standard and spiked serum samples with a detection limit in the order of sub nanomolar concentration levels.

A schematic of the developed immunosensing technique is depicted in Fig. S1. The details of materials, equipment, and the generation, purification and characterization of anti-myoglobin antibodies are provided in the Supporting Information. The details of the important experimental procedures are elaborated in the next sub-sections.

The synthesis of GQDs was carried out using a waste graphite electrode (separated from a spent alkaline battery) as the starting material. The waste graphite electrode was washed with water, dried, and then milled to obtain a powder. The synthesis procedure was based on the modified Hummer's technique as per the experimental details described in the Supporting information (Section 4, SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of screen printed electrodes (SPEs) with GQDs and their subsequent bioconjugation with anti-cMyo antibody
ce:section.ce:para: Dilute dispersion of GQDs was made in a 10mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The working electrode area of the carbon SPEs was modified with GQDs by the bulk electrolysis process. 100µL of a diluted GQD solution (5X of the synthesized stock) were plunged onto the surface of the working electrode. Electrodeposition was then performed at a constant positive potential of 0.7V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for a period of 10min. After the electrodeposition process, the electrode surface was washed several times with DI water and PBS buffer.

In order to bioconjugate with the anti-cMyo antibodies, the GQD modified electrode (working electrode area) was incubated with a 50µL freshly prepared aqueous mixture of 400mM EDC and 100mM NHS (1: 1 v/v). This step was required for the activation of –COOH groups on GQDs surface. Counter and reference electrode areas were masked during this step. The GQD modified and surface activated electrode was then incubated (30min) with 20µL of anti-cMyo antibodies (25μg/mL anti-cMyo in 10mM PBS). The produced bioconjugated electrode was thoroughly rinsed with the Tween 20-PBS mixture (0.001% Tween 20, pH 7.4) to remove unbound antibody molecules. Its extended incubation with the BSA protein solution (0.5mg/mL in PBS buffer) for 30min ensured the blocking of non-specific antibody sites. The above developed immunosensor was finally washed with PBS buffer, dried under nitrogen gas, and stored at 4°C.

The cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry, linear sweep voltammetry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis was carried out with an electrochemical analyzer (CH instruments 660-D with multiplexer CHI 684-8). The details are given in Supporting Information (Section 5, SI).

The developed antibody immobilized GQDs immunosensor was tested for its EIS response with respect to varying myoglobin concentrations, i.e. 0.01–100ng/mL. The antigen solutions were prepared in PBS (pH 7.4). Myoglobin-spiked serum samples were also prepared in PBS buffer. A small volume of the antigen solution was exposed to the working electrode area of the immunosensor. It was then left to incubate for 10min to ensure the antibody-antigen binding. The electrode was then washed with PBS and dried under the N2 gas flow before placing it in an electrochemical cell (containing probe redox electrolyte) for EIS measurements. The limit of detection (LOD) of the developed immunosensor was determined by the equation: LOD=K×sd/S. The value of constant K was taken as 3.3, sd was the standard deviation of the blank sample, and S was the slope of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the generated anti-cMyo antibodies
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S2(a), the target antigen was recognized from both the sera and antibody samples with significantly high titer. The ELISA data on the binding assay showed a proportional increase in the absorbance values with the increasing myoglobin concentrations, thereby verifying the desired activity of the generated antibodies. The activity of the isolated and purified antibodies from the serum samples did not change during the different experimental steps. The changes in the absorbance signals were insignificant during the test of blank control and non-specific BSA (Fig. S2(b)) suggesting that the produced antibodies should only be specific to the target antigen (myoglobin). The results of UV–vis absorption and gel electrophoresis analysis of the generated antibodies are depicted in Fig. S3. The occurrence of a typical UV–vis absorption peak at around 280nm confirms the presence of aromatic amino acids in the structure of anti-myoglobin antibodies. Gel electrophoresis data indicated a characteristic band at 150kDa, which also revealed the successful production of purified anti-myoglobin IgG antibody. Bio-layer interferometer study also confirmed the binding efficiency of the produced antibody toward its target antigen (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis, FTIR, HR-TEM, FE-SEM, particle size and AFM characterizations of the synthesized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S1 shows the schematic of the proposed approach for the assembly of GQDs based label free impedimetric immunosensor. As mentioned in the experimental section, the synthesis of GQDs was processed from a waste graphite electrode of a spent alkaline battery. The proposed processing of a waste material has yielded high quality GQDs as evidenced by several characterization techniques.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1(a). A broad absorption profile is observed with the presence of a peak at around 340nm which can be assigned to the typical absorption of energy in the electronic π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (Pan et al., 2010a; Shen et al., 2011; Xie et al., 1999). The PL spectrum of the GQDs upon their excitation with 340nm is also shown in Fig. 1(a). The synthesized GQDs solution was a yellowish brown product under the normal daylight conditions. However, it was emitting the bright blue luminescence when excited with UV light (inset of Fig. 1(a)). A strong emission peak was observed at around 450nm characterized with a Stoke shift of around 110nm. The PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1(b). The fluorescence emission by GQDs can be accounted for by the radiative recombination of excitons which are trapped by the defects generated during the synthesis and functionalization steps (Bourlinos et al., 2008; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009; Ray et al., 2009; Sun et al., 2006; Yang et al., 2012). The above defects, while acting as the efficient excitation energy trap, might also be correlated with the presence of abundant –COOH functional groups at the surface of GQDs. The presence of nanosized sp2 domains causing the charge confinement along with the possibility of two or multiphoton active processes might also lead to the interesting PL properties of GQDs.

Structural and morphological features of the GQDs have been assessed instrumentally by the combination of various techniques such as: HR-TEM, FE-SEM, Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), and AFM investigations. HR-TEM analysis ( Fig. 2) reveals that the synthesized GQDs are well dispersed with the uniform diameter in the range of 5–7nm. HR-TEM image and corresponding Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)/Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) patterns (Fig. 2(c) and (d)) show crystalline hexagonal structure. FE-SEM analysis (Fig. S5) illustrates the formation of nanoaggregates of GQDs. Some individual GQDs of 5–7nm diameter are also observed. The corresponding EDX analysis (Fig. S6) highlights the presence of elemental carbon as the major component of the sample. A strong intensity of the observed peak indicates high relative purity of the synthesized GQDs. The estimated elemental ratio (%w) of carbon in the synthesized GQDs was around 94%.

The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of a well dispersed solution of GQDs is shown in Fig. S7(a). The sample was characterized with a PDI (poly-dispersity) index of 0.159 with the average hydrodynamic diameter of 38nm. Thus, the synthesized GQDs were largely monodispersed in their solution form. This property is particularly advantageous to exploit the applications of GQDs toward the nano-scale biosensors. As the DLS technique also takes in to account the bound solvation layers, the hydrodynamic size obtained by DLS technique is larger than the particle size determination from HR-TEM. The results of zeta potential analysis (shown in Fig. S7(b) with a negative potential value (ζ=−69.4mV) also verify the formation of –COOH functionalized GQDs. The topographic analysis of GQD by AFM is useful to determine the roughness profile of the sample film ( Fig. 3(a)). The line profile analysis of the prepared sample highlighted that the nanoparticles were in nicely dispersed form with the heights of the individual GQDs <10nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, XRD analysis and contact angle measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) of the GQDs with the absorption peaks at 1700 and 1400cm−1 indicates the presence of carboxylate groups, while that at 3420cm−1 corresponds to the vibrations of C–OH. The absorptions at around 2927 and 1370cm−1 are ascribed to C-H stretching, while the peak at 1630cm−1 indicates C=C stretching. The above FTIR information, thus provides a strong evidence for the presence of –COOH, C=C, C–O, C=O, and –OH functional groups on the synthesized GQDs. These groups are also responsible for a strong hydrophilic nature of the synthesized GQDs (Tang et al., 2012; Tian et al., 2009; Xu et al., 2004).

Raman spectroscopic analysis (Fig. 3(c)) determines the defects and degree of orderness/disorderness of the synthesized GQDs. The samples were prepared on pre-cleaned silicon wafers by spin-coating of a suitably concentrated dispersion. An observed Raman peak at 1355cm−1 (i.e., D band) is associated with the disorder induced scattering, which resulted from the imperfections or the destruction of sp2 network by sp3 bonded carbon atoms. The peak at 1580cm−1 (i.e., G band) corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a two dimensional hexagonal lattice. Raman spectrum also provides useful information about the fragmentation of graphite structure during the formation of GQDs (Li et al., 2012, 2013; Tetsuka et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2013). The presence of D band is negligible in graphite sample. However, its appearance in the GQD sample can be ascribed to the structural imperfections of –COOH groups and the partially disordered crystal structure due to the small sp2 cluster size. Thus, the value of IntensityD/IntensityG ratio can be used as a marker to identify the formation of desired GQDs. The ID/IG ratio for the synthesized GQDs was significantly higher (∼0.9) than that of Graphite (∼0.1) signifying the efficiency of the proposed synthesis method.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of graphite and synthesized GQDs are shown in Fig. S8. The GQDs showed a broader (002) peak centered at around 25.7 degrees. The interlayer spacing of the GQDs was also broader than that of graphite. The XRD peak for the GQDs was shifted to a low degree compared with the graphite, indicating that the interlayer spacing of GQDs was larger than that of graphite. This could be attributed to the oxygen-containing groups introduced during the exfoliation and oxidation stage of graphite (Qu et al., 2012). For the measurement of contact angle, a drop of de-ionized water was placed on the surface of electrodes. The water droplet was formed using automatic dosing system as shown in the snapshots (Fig. S9). The contact angle (θ) of the liquid drop on bare Graphite SPE was 70±2° which decreased to 55±1° after its modification with GQDs. These results thus suggest the hydrophilic characteristics of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of the of the synthesized GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The results of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) analysis derived using the synthesized QDs are depicted in Fig. 4(a). The CV characteristics for the GQDs show significant enhancement in the peak currents relative to the initial graphite material. This behavior is an indicative of a better electroactive nature of the synthesized GQDs. The LSV analysis of GQDs depicts the prominence of a reduction peak around −0.7V which is absent in the case of graphite. The appearance of this clear reduction peak is due to the reduction of functional group (e.g., –OH, –COOH) on the surface of the GQDs (Fig. 4(a), inset) (Sharma et al., 2013). These investigations, carried out in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 20.0μM FeII/FeIII, with a sweep rate of 2mV/s, also highlight the Tafel region (i.e. the rising part of the voltammogram) to explain the electron transfer kinetics between the graphite or GQDs and the redox probe. The chronoamperometry curve of the GQDs on a graphite electrode is depicted in Fig. 4(b). The electroactive nature of the GQDs was confirmed further through an observation of a typical bulk electrolysis behavior due to accumulation of the GQDs over the graphite electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of anti-cMyo antibodies bioconjugated GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption and Raman spectra of the anti-cMyo antibodies bioconjugated GQDs are shown in Fig. S10. The attachment of the antibody molecules on the GQDs surface is supported with the appearance of an antibody specific UV–vis absorption peak at 280nm as a result of absorbance by the aromatic ring portion of their protein structure. A broad and distinct Raman peak at around 2800–3000cm−1 is also typical for the vibrations of proteins (Surmacki et al., 2013). These investigations, along with the change in electrochemical impedance characteristics (discussed in the next section) have verified the successful desired grafting of the anti-cMyo antibodies onto the surface of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of screen printed electrodes during different steps of immunosensor development
ce:section.ce:para: Some introductory details of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are mentioned in the Supporting Information (Section 6) (Huang et al., 2011). Fig. 5(a) shows the impedance spectra recorded during different development stages of immunosensor. The semicircle diameter for the antibody conjugated GQDs was larger than those of both the bare and the GQD modified SPE as the processes of the electron transfer between the electrode and the redox probe were hindered by the immobilization of biomolecules. The values of Rct were deduced by modeling and fitting the obtained impedance data into Randle's equivalent circuit. The initial modification of the bare SPE electrode with GQDs resulted in an enhanced conductance value (Rct=9.52kΩ) as indicated by a lower Rct. The GQDs are well-known as the conducting tunnel enabling an improved kinetics of charge transfer between the electroactive surface and the redox probe. In contrast, the values of Rct increased after the immobilization of anti-myoglobin antibody and successive exposure of the bioelectrode to the tested antigen at different concentration levels. The observed value of Rct after the immobilization of antibody was around 17kΩ but were raised to around 31kΩ during the analysis of 100ng/mL myoglobin. A hindered diffusion of the redox probe toward the electrode surface could be associated with the insulating nature of both the antibody and antigen molecules which should have possibly acted as a definite kinetic barrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS detection of cardiac myoglobin (cMyo) with anti-myoglobin conjugated GQD/SPE
ce:section.ce:para: The developed immunosensor (Ab/GQD/SPE) has been utilized to quantify myoglobin antigen at various concentration levels (0.01–100ng/mL). A blank sample (free from target antigen cMyo) was taken as control to record the baseline value of Rct. The observed Nyquist plots for the tested antigen concentrations are shown in Fig. 5(a). The semicircle diameter was found to increase with the increasing antigen concentration. A plot of Rct value vs. the antigen concentration is shown in Fig. 5(b). A linear increase in the Rct value as a function of analyte concentration can be attributed to the presence of additional kinetic barrier molecules (antigen) as well as an increasing steric hindrance that limits the access of the redox probe to the electrode surface. The proposed Ab/GQD/SPE immunosensor is capable of detecting the target myoglobin antigen at ppb (ng/mL) concentration levels. The LOD was estimated as 0.01ng/mL from the linear calibration curve (Fig. 5(b)). This considerably improved sensitivity is mainly attributable to an effective immobilization of antibodies on the GQD SPE and an excellent electron mobility of the devised immunosensor. To further support the impedance data, the differential pulse voltammetry was also carried out for the detection of myoglobin. Fig. 6(a) shows the differential pulse voltamogram (DPV) recorded with Ab/GQD/SPE immunosensor in presence of cMyo. A reduction peak appeared at ~−0.62V (vs. Ag/AgCl) which is due to the reduction of iron moiety present in myoglobin. In general, Mb contains the heme group as an active site where heme -iron is in III oxidation state (ferric Fe3+) in native form which can be reduced directly at the electrode surface to its ferrous state (Fe2+ ) by one electron transfer (Dai et al., 2009). Our results indicate that single protonation of Mb accompanies the electron transfer from ferric state to the Antibody -functionalized nanostructured electrodes.

Fig. 6(b) shows the DPV as a function of myoglobin concentration and as the cMyo concentration increased, the current intensity of reduction peak also increased. The same concentration of myoglobin which has been used for impedance study is used for recording DPV. The dynamic linearity range was from 0.01ng/mL to 100ng/mL, following the linear equation y=1.342+0.04 (x) with R2 of 0.993. Further, the Ab/GQD/SPE immunosensor was also found specific towards cMyo even in the presence of other proteins, e.g. Troponin I, CK-MB, Hemoglobin, BSA, and Avidin (Fig. 5(c); Section 7, SI). The device was also found satisfactory reproducible (output variation (±2%) and it could also be regenerated for a minimum of five cycles (Section 8, SI; Fig. S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of myoglobin in spiked human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical utility of the developed immunosensor has been tested and demonstrated by analyzing some spiked human serum samples. For this practice, the serum samples of healthy subjects were spiked with known concentrations of myoglobin. 10µL of these spiked samples were exposed to the Ab/GQD/SPE electrode followed by the EIS analysis to evaluate corresponding Rct values. The results of the assay are given in Fig. 5(d). Acceptable recoveries (95–105%) of the spiked concentrations have been observed, thus suggesting that the proposed immunosensor can be practically used for the quantitative estimation of myoglobin in real samples. The limit of detection of the sensor is also almost similar (in the range of 0.01ng/mL) to that achieved with pure antigen solutions. It is thus concluded that the proposed Ab/GQD/SPE immunosensor should be used as a promising tool for the clinical diagnosis of cardiac myoglobin.

The proposed immunosensing platform has been developed on low-cost screen printed electrodes (SPE) using in-house generated and tested specific anti-myoglobin antibodies. The purified antibodies recognize the target antigen with significantly high titer. These antibodies have been used further for demonstrating a sandwich ELISA assay for myoglobin, which was characterized with a linear detection in a broad concentration range of 4.4–281ng/mL. The LOD of the above ELISA assay was estimated about 4ng/mL.

The immobilization of the above specific antibodies on GQD modified SPE has provided the development of a convenient and a highly sensitive immunosensor for cMyo. This EIS-based immunosensor has offered a LOD of 0.01ng/mL, which is almost 400 times improved relative to the ELISA tests. This performance of the proposed immunosensor is also comparable or better than earlier reported results (Table S3). The achievement of this improved sensitivity is also well suited for the point-of-care devices for cardiac management. The proposed bioelectrode is not only effective as a one-shot disposable strip, but can also be efficiently recycled for repeated use. The proposed EIS-based Ab/GQD/SPE immunosensor offers convenient, label-free, and precise analysis of the cardiac myoglobin in spiked human serum samples. With high sensitivity and specificity, this device can be used as an incisive diagnostic tool with the great potential to expand its applicability.

As an immobilized template, the GQDs offer some useful features over three different types of graphene (e.g., graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, and graphene). For instance, graphene oxide is non-conducting, while pure graphene bears no functional groups for biomolecule attachment. Functionalization of the aforementioned forms of graphene to create –COOH or –NH2 groups are a prerequisite to support the attachment of biomarkers but it may also affect the sensitivity of the material's electrochemical properties. In contrast, in the case of GQDs, the surface functionality is attained inherently during the product synthesis itself, which is a very important advantage over graphene structures.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present research work has been funded through CSIR, India grant (OMEGA/PSC0202/2.2.5) from CSIR, India. Satish Kumar Tuteja also thanks the CSIR, India his senior research fellowship. We are grateful to the Director, CSIR-CSIO, Chandigarh, India.

Online available Supporting Information contains details on materials, equipment, generation/purification/characterization of anti-cMyo antibodies, synthesis of graphene quantum dots, electrochemical measurements, the electrochemical behavior of screen printed electrodes during different steps of immunosensor development, investigation on the specificity of the developed immunosensor, and stability and regeneration of the developed immunosensor; along with Figs. S1-S11 and Tables S1-S3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.07.052.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
